story:
Waves crash over the deck. Captain Belanger shouts into the wind: “Grab that halyard. Release the line. Drop that sail. Brighten the lanterns.” Twilight slips away. The ship heels so hard the siderail buries under water. The rigging screams. Voices scatter across the deck—lines are tangled, a sailor is down. The masts groan. “Someone climb into the rigging before the masts break.” The first mate leans into the gale. “I’ll do it.” Doubt flickers—how can anyone climb in this darkness? But there’s no choice. A line is tied around his waist. He grips the swaying rope ladder.

“Rrrrrr—Boom.” The foremast cracks halfway up. Sails and spars whip loose. “Look out!” The broken mast slams the main mast, then splinters down, canvas thumping onto the deck. The thicker main mast holds. The wind snaps and whistles, the sea roars across the planks, and still the crew fights to save their ship.

Earlier that day, deep in the supplies hold, a hidden figure scratches pictures into dusty timber to pass the time. A stowaway. A young girl, hunched and quiet, listens to the hull sing—creaks and hushes, a rhythm of wood and water. She ran when pirates stormed her family’s boat in the harbor, jumped aboard this nearby vessel, and hid. Now she waits for the next port, planning to find a way back and learn what became of her people. Alone among sacks of dried food and water barrels, she speaks to the memory of her grandmother, the Wise One with sparkling eyes and long, robe-like hair. “You have the power of your imagination,” the remembered voice tells her. “Dream the future and call it.”

The ship shudders. The wind howls. Distant shouts from above. Something crashes. Fear rises, and then she chooses. “Grandmother would say to make a dream picture.” She draws a smooth sea and a boat gliding beneath a clear night. A crescent moon. Stars tossed like salt. She hums. She breathes slowly. She adds gentle waves with wiggly lines. Her shoulders drop. The rocking softens, or seems to. The waking dream takes hold.

Up on deck, the wind falters. The seas settle from rage to chop to hush. Sails stop snapping. The crew exhales as one. “We’re saved.” “We can still make the next port with the mainmast and mizzenmast.” They heave away torn canvas and broken spars. Captain Belanger scans the clearing horizon, baffled. “I’ve never seen it clear so fast. As if something magical made it happen.”

Across the sea, her family stares over water that is no longer wild. They fought off the pirates, but the child is missing. The grandmother’s lined face shines with calm. “I see her. She is safe, on a ship heading north to the next port.” The girl’s father grips the rail. “Cast off the lines. We’re going to find her.”

Between storm and hush, between fear and courage, a ship survives, a family holds faith, and a girl in the dark draws moonlight until the sea listens.
